Q1: I tried to set up a consistent study routine, aiming for about 30 hours a week spread across my courses. I'm a visual learner, so I'd block out time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps. Honestly though, my anxiety often disrupted my plans—I'd sit down to study and then feel this wave of fear about whether I was doing enough or understanding things correctly. Some weeks I stuck to my schedule, other weeks everything fell apart despite my intentions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I'd start each semester with a beautiful color-coded schedule, but then deadlines would pile up or I'd have extracurricular commitments, and suddenly I was cramming at odd hours. The anxiety made it worse because sometimes I'd avoid studying when I felt too scared about what I might not understand. Deadlines became my main driver rather than my planned routine.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements—because I was terrified of missing something important—or accessing video lectures. Sometimes I'd log in just to reassure myself that I hadn't missed a deadline, even if I'd already checked that day. The fear of falling behind kept me coming back, even when my motivation was pretty low. I also logged in when I wanted to participate in discussions or see what my peers were saying.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in to watch videos became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, have my coffee, and open the LMS to watch that day's lecture content—it was like checking social media but for school. The discussion forums also became routine because interacting with peers helped ease some of my anxiety about the material. Having those 14 peer interactions really helped me feel less alone in struggling with concepts.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best visually. I spent over 250 minutes watching them, often pausing to take visual notes or rewatching confusing sections. The discussion posts were also valuable because seeing other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts from different angles. When I was feeling scared about my understanding, reading through peer discussions reassured me that others had similar questions.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials that were just walls of text—my visual learning style made them feel overwhelming and hard to process. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skip them to save time. Honestly, my low motivation made it hard to push through anything that didn't feel immediately necessary or engaging. If something looked too dense, my anxiety would spike and I'd close the tab.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear visual component or when it directly connected to assignments or exams. I needed to feel like I was making tangible progress—like checking something off a list or understanding a concept I could visualize. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because the interaction with peers gave me immediate feedback and reduced my isolation. If something felt abstract or disconnected from assessment, my low motivation made it easy to skip.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Part of me wanted to do them to feel more prepared, but my anxiety made me question whether spending time on them was the right choice when required work was pending. Required activities had this weight of necessity that pushed through my low motivation, while optional ones just added to my stress about making the right choices. I often skipped optional things and then felt guilty about it later.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study offline, especially when I needed to create my visual notes without constant internet distractions. I used textbooks and created physical mind maps and diagrams that helped me see connections between concepts. My extracurricular activities sometimes meant studying on the go with printed materials or downloaded PDFs. A lot of my actual learning happened with my colored pens and paper, not on the LMS.
Q10: When I was creating my visual study materials—diagrams, flowcharts, color-coded notes—none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd spend hours synthesizing information from multiple sources into one comprehensive visual, but the LMS only saw that I'd watched a video once. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person or over video calls, completely invisible to the platform. My best learning moments were often when I was explaining concepts to myself out loud while drawing them out.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video lecture once on the LMS, download the slides, and then spend hours working with those materials offline. My visual learning process required me to transform the content into my own diagrams and notes, which I couldn't do effectively while logged in. Sometimes I'd access a reading once just to download it, then study it extensively on my tablet without internet connection. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the real engagement.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked so high that even logging in felt overwhelming. When I had multiple deadlines converging with my extracurricular commitments, I'd freeze up and avoid everything. My low resilience meant that one bad grade or confusing topic could spiral into days of avoidance. During those times, I felt scared and paralyzed, and the CBT sessions I had were meant to help but the fear was still pretty consuming.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use—when I felt overwhelmed, I'd either obsessively check the platform or avoid it entirely. Fatigue from my extracurriculars combined with 30 hours of intended study time meant I was often running on empty. Confusion about course material triggered my fear response, making me less likely to engage rather than more. Competing responsibilities forced me to triage, and sometimes the LMS activities that weren't immediately due got pushed aside.
Q14: There were times when I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, consumed by anxious thoughts about other deadlines or life stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd log in and click around aimlessly because I felt like I should be doing something but couldn't focus. Those 250 minutes of video watching weren't all quality attention—some of it was just background noise while I worried.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My anxiety created this paradox where the more important something was, the more scared I felt about doing it wrong, so I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better before starting, which was just procrastination in disguise. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was another matter entirely. My assignment completion rate being so low reflects this avoidance cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my anxiety-driven avoidance. I'd have low activity for weeks and then suddenly cram everything in the final days before something was due. The panic of an approaching deadline somehow felt more manageable than the ongoing dread of an open-ended task. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I functioned with my low motivation and high anxiety.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback could temporarily boost my mood and motivation, making me more likely to engage. But critical feedback, even constructive criticism, would trigger my anxiety and sometimes made me avoid the course for days. I'd obsessively check for grades and then feel devastated or relieved depending on the outcome. The fear of negative feedback sometimes kept me from submitting work at all, which explains my low completion rate.
Q18: The course structure with its ten online components gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my extracurriculars and study when my anxiety was lower. Bad because without rigid structure, my low motivation meant things slipped through the cracks. Having clear modules and deadlines helped, but when things were too open-ended, I'd flounder. The discussion forums being integrated helped because peer interaction was one of my more consistent engagement points.
Q19: The visual elements—video lectures especially—made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. Having discussion forums where I could interact with peers helped reduce my isolation and anxiety. However, the sheer volume of content across ten online courses was overwhelming and triggered my fear of falling behind. When course design assumed consistent, steady engagement rather than accommodating anxious students who work in bursts, it made things harder for me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in repeatedly due to anxiety, watching videos, participating in discussions, but then freezing when it comes to actually submitting work. The fear of being judged on assignments can be paralyzing even when you're technically 'engaged' with the content. High activity might also mean someone is struggling to understand and keeps revisiting materials without making progress. Or they might be doing extensive offline work that doesn't translate to submissions yet.
Q21: That somewhat reflects my experience, actually. My exam score was decent at 68.87 even though my attendance was low and my assignment completion was poor. I learned a lot offline through my visual study methods that didn't show up in the LMS. Some students are just efficient—they access what they need, process it deeply elsewhere, and perform well without constant platform activity. The LMS doesn't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity of clicks.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. They can reveal deadline-driven behavior versus consistent engagement. But they completely miss the emotional experience, the quality of attention, and all offline learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying from anxiety or while deeply focused. They miss the real understanding that happens when I'm drawing diagrams at my desk with downloaded materials.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when I'm genuinely curious and making connections between concepts, creating those visual maps in my head and on paper. For me, engagement also includes feeling connected to peers and the learning community, even when I'm scared. It's showing up mentally and emotionally, even when my anxiety makes it hard—which is different from just showing up in the data logs.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected every interaction with the course—the fear before clicking submit, the relief after checking a grade, the paralysis that kept me from completing assignments. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline visual learning process or the peer support that happened outside the platform. It can't show that I genuinely cared about learning despite my low motivation scores, or how hard I worked to manage my mental health while studying. My struggle and effort are invisible to the data.
